records.ety: [OE. strecchen, AS. streccan; akin to D. strekken, G. strecken, OHG. strecchen, Sw. sträcka, Dan. straekke; cf. AS. straeck, strec, strong, violent, G. strack straight; of uncertain origin, perhaps akin to E. strong. Cf. Straight.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stretched (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Stretching.]

defs: To reach out; to extend; to put forth.
quotes: And stretch forth his neck long and small.
I in conquest stretched mine arm.

defs: To draw out to the full length; to cause to extend in a straight line; as, to stretch a cord or rope.
To cause to extend in breadth; to spread; to expand; as, to stretch cloth; to stretch the wings.
To make tense; to tighten; to distend forcibly.
quotes: The ox hath therefore stretched his yoke in vain.

defs: To draw or pull out to greater length; to strain; as, to stretch a tendon or muscle.
quotes: Awake, my soul, stretch every nerve.

defs: To exaggerate; to extend too far; as, to stretch the truth; to stretch one's credit.
quotes: They take up, one day, the most violent and stretched prerogative.

defs: To be extended; to be drawn out in length or in breadth, or both; to spread; to reach; as, the iron road stretches across the continent; the lake stretches over fifty square miles.
quotes: As far as stretcheth any ground.

defs: To extend or spread one's self, or one's limbs; as, the lazy man yawns and stretches.
To be extended, or to bear extension, without breaking, as elastic or ductile substances.
quotes: The inner membrane . . . because it would stretch and yield, remained umbroken.

defs: To strain the truth; to exaggerate; as, a man apt to stretch in his report of facts.
To sail by the wind under press of canvas; as, the ship stretched to the eastward.
Act of stretching, or state of being stretched; reach; effort; struggle; strain; as, a stretch of the limbs; a stretch of the imagination.
quotes: By stretch of arms the distant shore to gain.
Those put a lawful authority upon the stretch, to the abuse of yower, under the color of prerogative.

defs: A continuous line or surface; a continuous space of time; as, grassy stretches of land.
quotes: A great stretch of cultivated country.
But all of them left me a week at a stretch.

defs: The extent to which anything may be stretched.
quotes: Quotations, in their utmost stretch, can signify no more than that Luther lay under severe agonies of mind.
This is the utmost stretch that nature can.

defs: The reach or extent of a vessel's progress on one tack; a tack or board.
Course; direction; as, the stretch of seams of coal.
